posts.summary: Muratori argues that Windows 8's closed app distribution model poses a serious long-term threat to software innovation. By requiring all modern UI apps to go through the Windows Store, Microsoft is establishing itself as sole gatekeeper for what software can run on Windows — a position that will become increasingly dangerous as the desktop interface is inevitably phased out. Drawing a direct parallel to the DOS-to-Windows transition of the 1990s, he predicts the classic desktop will be obsolete within 10-20 years, leaving users trapped in a censored ecosystem where today's most acclaimed games and culturally significant software would already be banned under current certification rules. He methodically demonstrates that Microsoft has almost no financial incentive to keep distribution closed, since they already allow third-party payment processing within apps. The piece concludes that Microsoft should open Windows 8's distribution model to differentiate from Apple's closed iOS and attract the developer support critical to competing in the tablet and phone markets.
key_points: Under Windows 8 certification requirements, every PC Game of the Year and most Emmy-nominated drama equivalents would be banned from the Windows Store due to content restrictions on violence, profanity, and adult themes.

The DOS-to-Windows transition took roughly 10 years to make DOS obsolete and 20 years to remove it entirely — the same timeline likely applies to the classic desktop being replaced by the Windows 8 modern UI.

Microsoft has no meaningful financial incentive to keep distribution closed because they already allow developers to use third-party payment systems for in-app purchases, meaning the store's distribution monopoly generates negligible revenue.
No company can predict the future well enough to write certification requirements that won't accidentally prohibit revolutionary innovation — the only safe policy is effectively open distribution.
Through exhaustive analysis of Microsoft's own documentation, there is no legal method for developers to distribute Windows Store apps to consumers without Microsoft's explicit digital permission.
money_quotes: This vision of a future Windows heavily censored by Microsoft is chilling. But how likely is it to actually occur?
In short, the desktop in Windows 8 is where MS-DOS was in Windows 3.0.

If you believe that history repeats itself, the answer is unambiguous: it will be relegated to obscurity in ten years, and it will cease to exist outside manually installed compatibility software in twenty.

Without accurate knowledge of the future, by definition the only way to avoid accidentally prohibiting innovation is to not meaningfully prohibit anything. So the only certification requirements Microsoft could draw up that would fully support the future would be ones that effectively certified anything developers could possibly create.
The fact that iOS is closed is actually the main reason why Windows 8 for tablets and phones must be open.
Experimentation on open platforms is one of the primary sources of innovation in the computer industry. There are no two ways about that.
Does Microsoft really want to go into that battle without some of their biggest assets?

Year after year of great new and previously unattainable things, brought to you by motivated, creative developers who were free to go wherever their vision took them, knowing full well that if they made something great, there was no barrier between them and disseminating it to the world.
posts.key_insight: A platform owner who controls software distribution must be able to perfectly predict the future of computing to avoid accidentally prohibiting innovation — since no company can do this, the only rational policy is open distribution.

posts.summary: Casey Muratori describes building 'Walk Monster,' a tool for testing and visualizing player movement code in The Witness. Starting from the need to bulletproof player collision so it never breaks immersion, he iterates through several spatial exploration algorithms — from Rapidly Exploring Random Trees to a custom flood fill to his final 'Localized Directional Sampling' approach — to map everywhere the player can go. Each algorithm reveals tradeoffs between sampling density, boundary accuracy, and computational efficiency. The tool immediately finds real bugs including walkable slopes that shouldn't be, phantom collision volumes, and one-way-traversible rocks. He concludes by outlining open questions about boundary probing, visualization, and plans to fix the movement code itself.
key_points: Player movement code must be invisible to maintain immersion — any collision bug (getting stuck, falling through floors, walking through walls) breaks the experience and can strand players
Testing must exercise the actual code path, not just analyze data; Muratori builds both synthetic input emulation and a DriveTowardPoint function to test at different levels of fidelity
Rapidly Exploring Random Trees are designed for high-dimensional pathfinding, not exhaustive coverage of low-dimensional spaces — a mismatch he recognized only after implementation
His 'Localized Directional Sampling' algorithm uses directional awareness (not just proximity) to avoid boundary echo and efficiently flood-fill continuous 3D walkable space
Even a rudimentary version of Walk Monster immediately found real shipping-quality bugs: unwalkable slopes that were walkable, invisible collision volumes, and one-way-only rock passages
money_quotes: The last thing you ever want to happen is to have the player notice the fact that they're sitting at a computer asking it to move a virtual viewpoint around.
When you're trying to test a system, the most important thing to do is actually test the system. Even though that sounds like a straightforward rule, people writing tests often fail to observe it.
But this is testing the data, not the actual code. It's still easily possible to have bugs in the movement code that result in bad behavior even with sanitized data.
The entire goal of an algorithm like this is to concentrate the samples in the areas where they are most likely to yield productive results.

Because this is a continuous space and only an infinite number of samples can truly reveal its exact shape, your ratio of meaningful to meaningless samples is literally the measure of how effective your algorithm is at producing the walkable area.
What to name my first source file. This is always the most important part of any project. If you have the right name for your source file, it's smooth sailing from there.
The Witness is meant to be a contemplative game, but wondering why there seemed to be a rock where there was no rock was not meant to be one of its koans.
If you play games often, you've probably encountered one or more of these problems in shipping titles, and you know what I'm talking about.
posts.key_insight: Effective testing requires exercising the actual system under test, not proxies for it, and algorithm selection must match the actual dimensionality and goals of your problem rather than the superficial shape of it.

posts.summary: Muratori chronicles debugging a five-second UI stall in The Witness caused by a low-level keyboard hook (SetWindowsHookEx) that blocked input processing during game loading. The hook was Microsoft's own recommended workaround for disabling Windows logo keys in fullscreen games, but it conflicted with Raw Input, which the game needed for mouse movement. After hours of experimentation, the fix turned out to be a single undocumented flag (RIDEV_NOHOTKEYS) during Raw Input initialization. Muratori uses this experience to argue that poorly designed platform APIs waste enormous aggregate developer time across the industry, turning what should be simple operations into hours of archaeology through broken documentation and conflicting subsystems.
key_points: Low-level keyboard hooks (WH_KEYBOARD_LL) require the registering thread to yield to Windows, causing input stalls when the thread is busy loading or halted by a debugger, with a default 5-second timeout before Windows silently removes the hook.

Microsoft's own recommended solution for disabling Windows logo keys (SetWindowsHookEx) was silently broken by the introduction of Raw Input, which processes keyboard events before the hook layer ever sees them.
The actual fix was the undocumented RIDEV_NOHOTKEYS flag, whose documentation falsely claims it only blocks application-level hotkeys, not system-level ones.
Muratori advocates a 'static discipline' for code: any modification should leave the code in equal or better stability than you found it, rather than just hammering until the new thing works.
Platform API quality has a massive leverage effect — every hour Microsoft spends on clear APIs saves thousands of hours across the industry, making it the opposite of a zero-sum game.
money_quotes: The great thing about programming on Windows is that it is the only commercially viable platform where you can ship software to users without getting approval from a giant bureaucracy.
Five seconds wasted every breakpoint is bad news, and people often underestimate the effects of frustration that can build up over time due to flaky and unpleasant development environments.
It's always best to fully understand a problem before you fix it. If you just patch over its symptoms but never figure out what the problem really was, it will often come back to haunt you.
Never, ever trust the documentation. It's always either inaccurate or incomplete.

The cost of obtuse, easy to misuse APIs is multiplied across all these projects, turning the hundreds of hours we will spend on The Witness platform work into hundreds of thousands of hours when you consider that this same work has to be done by every other developer, too.
The hours I spent tracking down this bug were hours that won't get spent making parts of The Witness better, and there's nothing me or the team can do about that.

If Microsoft started realizing that software on Windows gets better if their APIs are clear, concise, and difficult to misuse, they might start changing their development processes to make API quality a priority.
It's really thousands of zero-sum games being played, all across the industry, and the end result is universally worse software because the underlying platforms take up too much effort.
posts.key_insight: Poorly designed platform APIs impose a massive hidden tax on the entire software industry, as every team must independently spend hours debugging the same preventable problems that could be eliminated by investing in clear, well-designed interfaces.

posts.summary: Muratori recounts debugging a mysterious bug in The Witness's editor where a translation manipulator would fail to move objects along certain axes depending on camera angle. After methodically ruling out plane construction errors and axis-specific issues, he discovered the problem lay in the ray-versus-plane intersection function — specifically that it doesn't check whether the intersection occurs in front of or behind the camera. The post walks through the geometric reasoning behind translation manipulators, the debugging process, and ends as a cliffhanger challenge for readers to identify the bug themselves.
key_points: Working in an unfamiliar codebase is like moving to a foreign country with the same alphabet but a different language — you gradually build fluency by starting with isolated features and expanding outward

Good translation manipulators use a plane containing the drag axis oriented toward the camera, then project the cursor ray intersection onto that axis, avoiding the sticky feel of ray-vs-line approaches

The bug was camera-angle-dependent because the ray-plane intersection function returns a point even when the intersection is behind the camera (negative t value), causing erratic behavior at certain viewing angles
Debug rendering was the key diagnostic tool — drawing intersection points revealed they were closer to the camera than expected and not on the intended plane, pointing to the root cause
Even basic 3D math operations composed together can produce subtle bugs that are hard to catch by code inspection alone
money_quotes: Working in someone else's codebase is much like moving to a foreign country that happens to use the same alphabet as you do, but not the same language: technically, you can read, but you don't really have any idea what most things mean.
Normally, I do not want large rocks to move around. If I'm in the game engine, and a giant rock starts moving, I know I'm in for some debugging work.
Since even simple 3D math like this can be difficult to verify by inspection alone, my first inclination was to add some debugging rendering to verify that I was picking the dragging plane properly.
It was at that moment that my math brain finally woke up. It had been sleeping for about a decade, which is how long it'd been since I'd done any real mathematical programming.
The problem was embarrassingly obvious when you consider the clues I'd been generously given and yet completely ignored.
At this point, every serious 3D programmer who hasn't been doing other work for ten years surely knows what the bug was.
posts.key_insight: A ray-plane intersection that doesn't reject negative t values (intersections behind the camera) will produce erratic, camera-angle-dependent behavior that manifests as objects refusing to move along certain axes — a classic 3D math pitfall hidden inside the simplest possible geometric operations.

posts.summary: Muratori recounts debugging a transform manipulator bug in The Witness that he initially misdiagnosed as a floating point precision issue. After implementing a fix that worked but didn't fully make sense to him, he spent his Christmas break obsessing over why. Upon returning and stepping through the code, he discovered the real culprit: the plane library's get_normal() function silently normalized the a,b,c values, making them inconsistent with the d value computed during plane construction. The bug was fundamentally about mismatched assumptions between two programmers' mental models of what a 'plane' object means.
key_points: A bug fix that works but doesn't make theoretical sense is a red flag worth investigating — the 'precision improvement' fix succeeded only by accident, by avoiding the buggy code path entirely
The real bug was an API mismatch: plane_from_point_and_normal stored raw values while get_normal() silently normalized them, causing the normal and d value to be inconsistent
Different programmers carry different implicit assumptions about mathematical objects — one sees a plane as a geometric object with unit normals, another sees it as raw equation coefficients
Experienced programmers rely on consistent mental models to work at speed, and bugs are the inevitable cost when two different consistent models collide in the same codebase
Reconstructing a bug methodically — finding entity data, writing test programs, stepping through code — is more reliable than intuition alone
money_quotes: Sometimes, when there's a lot to do, you'll live with a bug fix because it works, rather than because it makes sense.
Clearly, the fix just plain shouldn't have worked. But it did.
To my complete surprise, it turned out to have absolutely nothing to do with precision.

If you are thinking of the plane as a geometric object, of course the normal is unit length, what else would it be?? But if you are thinking of it as some specific equation all the time, then it is natural to care about the a, b, c, you put in.
I honestly don't believe that this sort of thing is a case of us being presumptuous or sloppy; it's just the price you have to pay for being able to program complex things at a reasonable speed.
Ask yourself, if you had encountered this bug, and you had 'fixed' it as I had, would you have gone back and figured out what was really happening before moving on to something else?

The good news: congratulations to those of you who posted 'floating point precision' as a suggestion for the cause of the bug. You aren't any dumber than I am. The bad news: we're all dumber than we should be.
posts.key_insight: A bug fix that works for the wrong reasons is a ticking time bomb — the real virtue of a programmer is the compulsion to understand why a fix works, not just that it does.

posts.summary: Muratori describes his quest to solve what he calls 'The Nebraska Problem' — visible linear gaps that appear in blue noise patterns used for grass placement in The Witness. After multiple failed attempts using modified blue noise, gap targeting, relaxation, and directional constraints, he realizes that randomness itself is the obstacle: no random scheme can produce optimal coverage. He ultimately solves the problem with a deterministic pattern based on staggered concentric circle intersections, which provides better visual coverage with fewer points than blue noise. The key insight is that when optimality is the goal, you may need to abandon randomness entirely in favor of ordered, geometric approaches.
key_points: Blue noise patterns used for grass placement suffer from 'The Nebraska Problem' — accidental collinear alignments that create visible gaps from certain viewing angles, similar to corn rows seen from the road

Multiple randomness-based fixes failed (packing ratio changes, gap filling, line-of-sight planting, selective removal, relaxation, directional constraints) because the problem is inherent to random distributions, not a bug in any specific algorithm

The solution required two axes of curvature — intersecting concentric circles with staggered rings — a completely deterministic pattern that eliminates linear gaps while using fewer points than blue noise
Randomness is never optimal: for any specific set of criteria, a random approach will almost never find the optimal solution, and constraining randomness still won't get you there
The deeper lesson is recognizing when a problem is about optimization versus exploration, and being willing to abandon randomness when it becomes the limiting factor
money_quotes: If there's one thing I can say has been true throughout my entire programming career, it's that it has always paid to ask why I was doing things the way I was doing them.
There's nothing wrong with our practice, in this case. It's our theory that is apparently inadequate.
Randomness is never optimal. For any set of criteria, the chances that you will pick a set of optimal inputs in a randomized way, no matter how you constrain them, are extremely low.

I kept looking for schemes that would produce optimality out of randomness, but that's simply too unlikely. Because blue noise is a pretty good random pattern for my purposes, I was dancing around the breaking point of randomness, and every scheme I tried was destined for failure.
In order to make that leap, I had to switch from chaos to order, and drive at the optimum directly.
Even thoroughly entrenched ideas are often wrong, and questioning those ideas leads to surprising and important insights.
If I have an inkling that there is a 'better' lurking behind the 'good enough', I usually find it's worth it to push.
posts.key_insight: When optimizing for a specific criterion, randomness is inherently limiting — sometimes you must abandon stochastic approaches entirely and drive at the optimum with deterministic, geometric solutions.

posts.summary: Muratori follows up on his previous article about staggered concentric intersection patterns for grass placement, responding to reader questions. He provides the actual code used to generate the pattern, addresses scalability concerns about extending the pattern to larger areas, and explores potential approaches for making it tile infinitely. He also demonstrates that the scalloping artifacts are nearly invisible when rendered against a realistic ground texture rather than a high-contrast white background, and investigates Fermat spirals as an alternative pattern. He concludes by clarifying that this is not a grass simulation but an area-coverage problem.
key_points: The staggered concentric intersection pattern holds up surprisingly well at higher densities, with the staggering making it more robust than expected as point count increases
Three potential approaches for infinite tiling: regularizing a pleasing subset into something periodic, investigating non-circular curves like sine waves, or tiling the pattern with seam fixups
The scalloping artifacts that concerned readers are nearly imperceptible when rendered against a ground texture instead of a high-contrast white background, even from elevated viewpoints
Fermat spiral / sunflower patterns suffer from the same 'curved cornrow' artifacts as non-staggered concentric intersection, though a stagger fix might theoretically be possible
This is fundamentally an area-coverage problem, not a grass simulation — real grass growth patterns are irrelevant because the goal is hiding gaps in sparse coverage, not mimicking nature
money_quotes: Better yet, if one were a super duper programmer — like, we're talking healthcare.gov-level programmers here — you could even change the for loops to properly compute the starting and ending radii from the first intersection with the sample region.
The problem with circles is that they have a finite size, so they necessarily induce change on a scale related to their radii.
My guess is that this would be very difficult, because 'the problem with curvature is that it's always curving', for lack of a better way to say it.

I admit that these activities did not advance the state of the art in computer graphics as much as if I had actually sat down and done some work, but at the same time, these games aren't exactly going to play themselves, people!
I apologize for not remembering the other people who commented, but let's face it, it's really your responsibility to be a memorable person, and obviously you failed at that.
It's simply trying to figure out a way to best cover an area without you noticing the places that aren't covered.
I took my camera outside and took pictures of various grasses around town myself, and looked at the clumping and clustering.
posts.key_insight: The staggered concentric intersection pattern for grass placement is more robust than expected at high densities and its artifacts are nearly invisible against realistic ground textures, though extending it to infinite coverage remains an open problem requiring either periodic regularization, alternative curves, or tiling with seam fixups.

posts.summary: Muratori describes several technical features he built for The Witness's grass planting system, including variable-distance blue noise distribution, obstacle avoidance via raycasting, and edge-distance control using signed distance fields. He created a minimal test world called 'CaseyTown' to achieve fast iteration times, since the full game's asset streaming made startup slow. Despite building the system under deadline pressure for the PS4 announcement trailer, none of his grass improvements were actually needed for the trailer — though artists later adopted the features for production. The post illustrates how real production use exposed gaps in the initial feature set, particularly around controlling grass behavior at obstacle boundaries.
key_points: Fast turnaround times are critical: Muratori built a minimal test world ('CaseyTown') specifically to avoid the full game's slow asset-loading startup, keeping the change-to-test cycle under ten seconds

Variable blue noise spacing works by giving each point a radius instead of using a global minimum distance — proximity checks use the sum of two radii, making the algorithm naturally extensible to spatially varying density

Obstacle boundary control required planting grass twice: a prepass with random samples builds an approximate signed distance field to boundaries, then the real planting pass queries that distance to adjust parameters near edges
Raycasting for enclosure testing is inherently unreliable with non-watertight meshes, which is acceptable for grass planting but would need more robust solutions for critical gameplay systems
The grass system built under PS4 trailer deadline pressure had zero impact on the trailer itself, but eventually proved valuable when artists adopted it months later for production use
money_quotes: In my mind, one of the most important things on any project is minimizing the amount of time it takes for a change to be testable. Ideally, it should take less than ten seconds for a programmer to go from a changed source file to a running executable.

Everyone knows that when you write programming articles, you are supposed to make yourself sound very smart and capable, carefully omitting all the stupid things you did and the mistakes you made so it sounds as if you just showed up, pulled some spectacular code out of your… pocket… and great celebration ensued.
The actual impact on the PS4 trailer from my upgraded grass system was quite literally non-existent.
No longer safe in the confines of CaseyTown, where the grasses grow freely and feed the spotted unicorns, the 'fancy' grass system found out that life on the full Witness island was more demanding.
The actual boundary of the planted grass isn't known until after the grass is already planted, so it's a bit difficult to factor that into the distribution.
Not seeing the potential for anything clever, I decided to fall back on a brutish solution: plant the grass twice.

Raycasting, as handy as it is, is not exact. Unless all your meshes are watertight, and your ray-triangle intersection code completely robust (which it rarely is in a game), it is entirely possible for you to raycast right through one side of a solid object without actually hitting anything.
posts.key_insight: Practical game development solutions are often pragmatic rather than elegant — planting grass twice to discover boundaries, accepting imperfect raycasts, and building features that miss their original deadline but prove valuable later.

posts.summary: Muratori describes his work fixing the grass planting system in The Witness ahead of the PS4 unveiling trailer. When artists complained about 'huge gaps' in scattered geometry, he first added debug visualization rather than diving into the algorithm code. This revealed that the fern meshes were off-center in their source art, causing gaps that had nothing to do with the distribution algorithm. After fixing the mesh centering, smaller gaps remained, leading him to investigate the actual scattering algorithm. The post illustrates how debug visualization tools can quickly expose the real cause of visual problems before any algorithmic work begins.
key_points: When humans say they want 'random' distribution, they actually expect constant-density placement without clumps or gaps, which is a very specific non-random property
Adding debug visualization code early pays dividends across the entire debugging process, not just for the hardest bug
The 'huge gaps' problem turned out to be caused by off-center mesh geometry, not the distribution algorithm — a root cause nobody had identified despite extensive discussion
Geometry scattering tools are essential in game development because manually placing high-quantity elements like grass would be prohibitively expensive
Debugging in a simplified test area rather than the full game environment dramatically speeds iteration
money_quotes: When humans want something 'randomly' scattered, what they really expect to see is actually a very specific distribution that maintains a relatively constant density.
Unfortunately for the programmer, throwing your hands up and saying, 'but huge gaps can happen in truly random distributions!' is not generally looked upon as a reasonable response.
Instead of stepping through the code or even carefully reading it to see what it was doing, I did what I often do these days and added debug visualization code right away.

Why wait for the really hard bug before adding the debug code? It takes the same amount of time to write debug code earlier as it does later, but earlier means you can use it to speed debugging of all those not-as-hard bugs along the way.
Without enough visual information being displayed, people were free to ascribe problems to the most likely culprits.
Fixing this improved the problem they were observing with the 'huge gaps', without me ever even touching the algorithm. Chalk up another victory to early implementation of debug drawing code.
Humans are doing something very nonrandom when they place things this way, in fact: they are looking at and considering where each thing is relative to the other things they've already placed.
posts.key_insight: Debug visualization should be the first step in diagnosing visual problems, because making intermediate state visible often reveals that the real cause is entirely different from what everyone assumed.

posts.summary: Muratori wraps up his grass rendering series by introducing the mathematical concept of interpolation through an experimental video format rather than text. He found that text was inadequate for explaining the visual and diagrammatic nature of interpolation, so he recorded himself drawing on a notebook. The video covers linear blending, barycentric coordinates, and Bezier curves, though he ran out of time to cover bilinear interpolation and higher-degree Bezier constructions. He reflects positively on the video tutorial format and teases the next Witness Wednesdays topic: a case study on building a feature-rich editor panel with minimal code.
key_points: Some programming concepts, especially visual/mathematical ones like interpolation, are better taught through video with diagrams than through text articles
Bilinear interpolation, Bezier curves, and barycentric coordinates all reduce to repeated applications of the same simple linear blending function b(t)
Higher-degree Bezier curves can be constructed by simply adding or removing rows from the pyramid method used for linear and cubic cases
The next series will be a case study in building a feature-rich editor panel quickly and with minimal code, emphasizing practical code structure
money_quotes: There's too much stuff to talk about, even if I leave a lot out, and it's too hard to emphasize the important parts when I can't easily point at parts of diagrams.
I was going to show how bilinear interpolation is just applying the b(t) function three times, and how it boils down to the same weighted sum equation as well.
I would have liked to have gone fast enough to get that in there so it could just reinforce the notion that this equation is everywhere.
I think it would make a good case study for how to put a lot of features into something quickly and without a lot of code.
I'll start walking through how I went about writing it, and show a lot of examples of how I structured the code so it could be built quickly without becoming excessively janky.
posts.key_insight: The core mathematical concept of linear blending (interpolation) is a single equation that appears everywhere in graphics — from Bezier curves to bilinear texture sampling to barycentric coordinates — and understanding this unifying pattern is more valuable than memorizing each application separately.

posts.summary: Casey Muratori explains the concept of colored noise (white, blue, red, green) and its practical importance in procedural content placement, specifically in The Witness's grass planting system. He traces his own education from a 2005 conversation with rendering researcher Don Mitchell, who introduced him to blue noise patterns. The post dissects how The Witness's grass planter used naive Poisson disk sampling, which suffers from catastrophic computational costs as valid placement regions shrink, leading to visible gaps. He then describes a wavefront-based directed sampling approach (similar to Bridson's algorithm) that dramatically improves efficiency by only searching neighborhoods of existing points rather than the entire space. The post promises a follow-up addressing a subtler theoretical problem with the algorithm.
key_points: Blue noise patterns have high-frequency randomness but no low-frequency clumps or gaps, inspired by the arrangement of light-sensitive cells in human eyes, making them ideal for sampling and procedural placement.

Naive Poisson disk sampling (reject points too close to existing ones) has a catastrophic cost curve: as space fills up, the ratio of rejected to accepted samples explodes, making it computationally infeasible to fill the last gaps.

The fix is directed sampling via a wavefront approach: instead of picking random points across the entire space, only sample in the neighborhood (distance r to 2r) of existing frontier points, which is the same insight that solved Walk Monster's pathfinding problem.
In The Witness, the cost was even worse because each candidate point required a 3D raycast against world geometry before it could be evaluated, making rejected samples extremely expensive.

Learning about noise distributions fundamentally changed how Muratori approached all procedural placement code, not just rendering — recognizing that the type of random distribution matters as much as the randomness itself.
money_quotes: Most programmers don't know much about randomness beyond how to call the random number generator in the standard library of their favorite programming language.
As counterintuitive as it may seem, the way you fix this is by using an irregular sampling pattern.
Although we normally think of noise as undesirable, in this case it's actually what we need to prevent the eye from being drawn to the regularly changing pattern produced by regular sampling.
Once I saw the blue noise patterns, I immediately realized that I had been doing a lousy job on all the procedural placement code I'd ever done.
The more time you spend sampling and resampling wide open regions, the less time you spend mapping the actual edges of that region, which is the information you actually wanted.
Your ratio of meaningful to meaningless samples is literally the measure of how effective your algorithm is at producing the walkable area.

We are picking random points across an entire space that we want to fill, but as the possible valid places in that space become smaller and smaller, we are not adjusting our sampling to account for it.
posts.key_insight: Naive random sampling becomes catastrophically inefficient as valid regions shrink; directing samples to the frontier of already-placed points transforms an intractable problem into a tractable one, a principle that applies far beyond graphics.

posts.summary: Muratori advocates for 'semantic compression' as a programming methodology, where code is written straightforwardly first, then iteratively compressed by extracting shared patterns into reusable structures and functions — similar to how a dictionary compressor finds redundancy. He demonstrates this through a real example from The Witness's editor UI code, showing step-by-step how repetitive layout and button code was compressed into a clean Panel_Layout abstraction. He argues this bottom-up approach is vastly superior to object-oriented methodologies that try to design class hierarchies before writing any working code, because starting with concrete details and compressing upward naturally produces well-fitting, easy-to-use abstractions.
key_points: Never write reusable code until you have at least two concrete instances of the same pattern — premature reusability leads to poorly fitting abstractions
Good programming is like dictionary compression: repeatedly find semantic redundancy in working code and extract it into shared structures and functions
Objects should emerge naturally from compression of working procedural code, not be designed up-front through class hierarchies or UML diagrams
Development efficiency should be measured holistically across the entire lifetime of code: typing, debugging, modifying, adapting, and integrating

Experienced programmers rarely share how they actually write code because the process feels too straightforward to be worth posting about, leaving educational materials dominated by bad OOP methodology
money_quotes: I like to think of efficiency as holistically as possible. If you look at the development process for a piece of code as a whole, you won't overlook any hidden costs.
My mantra is, 'make your code usable before you try to make it reusable'.
I like 'compress' better as an analogy, because it means something useful, as opposed to the often-used 'abstracting', which doesn't really imply anything useful. Who cares if code is abstract?

The fallacy of 'object-oriented programming' is exactly that: that code is at all 'object-oriented'. It isn't. Code is procedurally oriented, and the 'objects' are simply constructs that arise that allow procedures to be reused.
Starting with the details and repeatedly compressing to arrive at the eventual architecture avoids all the pitfalls of trying to conceive the architecture ahead of time.
I spend exactly zero time thinking about 'objects' or what goes where.

If good programmers don't start posting about how to do good programming, we'll never get out of this nasty place where everyone has to go through six years of writing horrible object-oriented programs before they realize they're wasting their time.
I was introduced to 'object oriented programming' when I was 18, and it took me until I was about 24 to realize it was all a load of horseshit.
posts.key_insight: Good abstractions emerge naturally from compressing working code that has real duplication, not from designing class hierarchies before any code exists.

posts.summary: Muratori continues his series on compression-oriented programming by demonstrating how to progressively compress UI code from The Witness editor while maintaining 'continuous granularity' — ensuring that higher-level convenience functions never eliminate access to lower-level ones. He shows how naively modifying a push_button() function to handle toggle booleans would create a 'granularity discontinuity' that makes intermediate use cases awkward, and instead advocates adding a new bool_button() layer on top. He argues that programmers should resist the temptation to over-engineer solutions to language limitations, focusing on total cost rather than abstract notions of code cleanliness. He also clarifies that his critique of object-oriented programming targets the practice of orienting design around objects, not the incidental existence of object-like structures, and pushes back on claims that compression-oriented programming doesn't scale to teams.
key_points: Continuous granularity means ensuring that as you build higher-level abstractions, lower-level access points remain available, avoiding 'holes' in the API where users are forced to drop down multiple levels to accomplish intermediate tasks.

When compressing code, adding a new higher-level function (bool_button) is preferable to modifying an existing mid-level function (push_button), because modification can destroy a useful intermediate abstraction layer.

Programmers should resist over-engineering workarounds for language limitations — accepting a slightly ugly but simple solution (like pre-specifying column count) beats introducing complex machinery that increases total cost.
The rule of thumb for good API design: never supply a higher-level function that can't be trivially replaced by a few lower-level functions doing the same thing.

Compression-oriented programming naturally produces continuous granularity because you build upward from low-level code, whereas object-oriented programming requires you to explicitly design granularity in advance.
money_quotes: It's OK to accept a solution that's a little ugly if the alternatives are uglier.
You must always make sure that the primary thing you're evaluating in your head, when you make decisions in places where there are tradeoffs, is the total cost and only the total cost.
Never supply a higher-level function that can't be trivially replaced by a few lower-level functions that do the same thing.

Since compression-oriented programming means building the code by progressively bundling pieces of code into functions that operate at higher and higher levels, so as long as you just don't delete the smaller pieces as you build bigger ones, you automatically end up avoiding granularity discontinuities.
It is the practice of thinking that objects are important (or that they're even worth thinking about at all) that is wrong, and is best avoided.
Writing code for yourself to use is no different than writing code for someone else to use.
These are the kind of tradeoffs that are extremely annoying, and which exist only because the people who write the C++ spec have no idea what production programming is or how to do it properly.
If the total cost is going to be worse, it really doesn't matter what else you might say about the code philosophically. You still made the wrong decision.
posts.key_insight: Good API design requires continuous granularity — building higher-level convenience functions as layers on top of lower-level ones rather than replacing them, so users are never forced to jump between extreme abstraction levels to accomplish intermediate tasks.

posts.summary: Muratori examines the seemingly trivial decision of defining a C++ enum for a UI panel mode in The Witness editor, using it as a springboard to critique object-oriented class hierarchies. He argues that a simple enum with a count sentinel provides zero-initialization, easy iteration, trivial serialization, and flexible code organization — all of which a class hierarchy would make harder. He demonstrates that OOP's code segregation into separate files and virtual functions creates O(n) maintenance costs for common changes like adding parameters, while providing O(1) only for changes to individual object internals — which are actually the least frequent modifications. The post concludes that the sheer depth of reasoning behind even seven lines of code illustrates why programming well is genuinely hard.
key_points: A count sentinel at the end of an enum enables trivial iteration and array sizing, while zero-default initialization eliminates entire classes of bugs without constructors
Class hierarchies often end up reimplementing the enum they were supposed to replace, since serialization and factory patterns require stable IDs anyway
OOP's code segregation turns O(1) function signature changes into O(n) changes across base and derived classes, while providing no reciprocal reduction in cost for adding new types
The real benefit people attribute to OOP — preventing misuse — is actually about opaque API boundaries, which can be achieved with forward-declared structs and don't require class hierarchies
Artificially separating code into objects where no natural boundaries exist actively harms maintainability and legibility
money_quotes: The sad truth is that most of the time, if you think you are going to replace something simple like an enum that's used to control a mode with a class hierarchy where you have one class per mode, you often end up implementing the enum scheme inside it anyway.

This kind of code segregation often goes by positive-sounding names, depending on the particulars, such as 'abstraction', 'encapsulation', 'division of responsibility', etc. But what it really should be called is 'obfuscation', because that's the one thing that it definitely always does.
What you get when you structure things in the virtual dispatch way is an O(n) instead of O(1) cost for changing functions. That might be interesting if you got something back, but you really don't.

It is not worth trying to 'prevent mistakes' if you are also preventing good, maintainable code! Preventing mistakes should only be a focus if you know you can do so without producing bad code as a result.

Much of the time, you actually want code to be miscible. Most parts of your program really do depend on each other, and it makes the code extremely difficult to manage if you try to artificially separate it into 'objects' where there are no natural lines of separation.
The fact that I can write so much about such a small piece of code is a good example of why it's hard to learn how to program well, and also why good programmers don't always write good programs.
I basically never use constructors for this purpose. Instead, I try very hard to ensure that all my code always uses 0 as the proper initial value for everything.
posts.key_insight: Simple enums with zero-default conventions provide more flexibility, less typing, and better maintainability than class hierarchies, which impose O(n) costs on the most common code changes while optimizing for the least frequent ones.

posts.summary: Casey Muratori walks through implementing the type filtering system for the Lister Panel in The Witness's editor tools. He starts with a simple update function that loops over all entities and checks them against a filter, then builds out the filter using a fixed-size boolean array indexed by entity type. He explains his decision to use a fixed array over a dynamic one due to unfamiliarity with The Witness codebase's utility classes, and demonstrates his practice of writing usage code before finalizing an API. The post illustrates compression-oriented programming by showing how a single set_all_types_to helper function composes with direct array access to cover every needed operation.
key_points: When working in an unfamiliar codebase, prefer simple, foolproof constructs (fixed arrays) over flexible ones whose semantics you don't fully understand
Writing usage code before implementing an API forces you to think through what operations you actually need and reveals the right abstraction naturally
A single helper function (set_all_types_to) combined with direct array access composes to handle every plausible operation with minimal code
Editor code often benefits from 'banking' settings into a separate struct, enabling easy mode-switching and bulk state swaps
Adapting to a codebase's existing conventions (macros, naming, utilities) reduces maintenance burden for the team that owns the code
money_quotes: A lot of programmers — including myself, more times than I care to admit — end up plowing through these situations by just guessing at what the interface should be and then moving on.

The much smarter thing to do is to take the time to actually write usage code if you don't have it available. It's not as good as actually having a completely working system you can compress, but it's the next best thing, and much better than guessing.
Fixed arrays have the wonderful benefit that, as long as you know you never write off the end, they are totally foolproof and there are no surprises.
The right thing to do usually just pops right out at you once you take the time to do this.
Since I knew it was going to be necessary, I just saved myself some typing and put them in there to begin with.
In a foreign codebase, where I'm not so well versed, I don't always know what all the assumptions are.

Compression-oriented programming is always easy when you have a large set of compression options that you've mastered, and a bunch of code that does what you want. But often times during development, even if you have the former, you don't necessarily have the latter.
posts.key_insight: Writing out concrete usage code before designing an API reveals the right abstraction naturally and prevents over-engineering, especially in unfamiliar codebases where simpler constructs reduce risk.

posts.summary: Muratori describes implementing special filters for The Witness's Lister Panel editor UI. He walks through the design process of creating a table-driven filtering system that handles three-state results (pass, fail, not applicable), then extends it with parametric filters using an integer parameter to avoid writing redundant per-flag filter functions. He further piggybacks editing capability onto the same filter functions, allowing users to both filter and modify entity properties through the same code path, culminating in bulk editing across all listed entities.
key_points: Filter systems that operate on heterogeneous entity types need three-state logic (true, false, not applicable) rather than simple booleans, so inapplicable filters don't incorrectly exclude entities
Table-driven dispatch is preferred over switch statements when you need to attach metadata (like display names) to each case and iterate over entries from multiple callsites
A macro defining the function signature in one place keeps all filter functions and the typedef synchronized with zero maintenance cost
Adding a parameter to filter table entries enables generic filters (like flag testing) that avoid writing one function per flag value
Piggy-backing editing onto filter functions reuses existing type-checking and data access logic, keeping the code concise and avoiding duplication
money_quotes: The Lister Panel is a place that is all about intrusively looking at things and making decisions about them, and it is clear and understandable to put the code that does this determination all in one place.

If instead these tests are scattered throughout the entity classes as a suite of virtual functions, suddenly it becomes very complicated for someone reading the code to know, say, what constitutes a layer, or even how they would go about modifying what that meant if they needed to.
Switch statement vs. table is always a milquetoast decision, because the two are extremely similar and most of the time it's not clear that there's a right answer.
This is one of my favorite types of macros, because it saves a ton of typing while not really costing much in terms of legibility or debugging clarity.

It turned out to be very easy to piggy-back editing on top of filtering, just by passing more arguments to the filters. Since the filter code knows how to deal with whatever it is filtering, it is usually quite easy to add code for changing whatever it is filtering when necessary.
So, with very little code, I now had an entity list with configurable filters and configurable buttons for editing any of the filterable parameters where that made sense.
posts.key_insight: By designing filter functions that both query and modify entity properties through the same code path, you can build a flexible editor UI with filtering, per-entity editing, and bulk editing from very little code.

posts.summary: Muratori describes implementing hierarchical group display in The Witness editor's Lister Panel, which previously only supported flat entity lists. He walks through the design decisions involved in recursive tree traversal, where to store expansion state, and how to implement filtering that preserves hierarchy. The post covers practical considerations like stack space in recursive functions, why UI state shouldn't live on domain objects, and an efficient algorithm for pulling parent groups into filtered views. Along the way, he shares a real war story about a recursive function that overflowed the stack in debug mode. The technical content is wrapped in an elaborate running joke about Chronicles of Narnia references.
key_points: Recursive tree traversal is appropriate when you can verify the recursion depth is bounded, but local variables multiply stack usage per frame — seemingly small buffers can cascade into gigabytes of stack consumption
UI-specific state like tree expansion should be stored on the UI component (via a hash set), not on domain entities, to avoid bloating the entity class and coupling it to presentation concerns

API design involves tradeoffs between common and uncommon cases — he chose the expansion_button() API that was cleanest for the common boolean-storage case even though the hash-table case was slightly uglier

Hierarchical filtering requires a prepass that walks filtered entities up to their root ancestors, similar to a disjoint set forest, ensuring each entity is visited only once while pulling in all necessary parent groups
money_quotes: The problem with the internet is that everyone thinks they're an expert. That would be fine, if everyone actually was an expert, but the truth is that only I am an expert, so it's just frustrating.
Every time you call a function, all of its locals get pushed onto the program's stack. So a recursive call acts like a multiplier on the stack space required to execute the function.
Sometimes your code can end up being used in more extreme circumstances than what you consider appropriate.
Whether or not a group entity is expanded in the Lister Panel isn't really something that should be stored on the entity itself.

As a policy, it invites disaster by suggesting that everyone who ever needs to do anything with entities should just dump that data into the entity itself, which bloats the class and takes up a lot of space unnecessarily.
Filters are usually best written on non-groups even if you want to manipulate the groups themselves.
Present Me suspects that Past Me was writing this code in a hurry and either didn't realize the loops could fold together, or more probably, didn't want to spend the extra time to do so.
posts.key_insight: Practical UI programming requires careful decisions about where state lives and how traversal algorithms handle filtering — keeping UI state out of domain objects and using efficient ancestor-walking algorithms avoids both architectural bloat and performance pitfalls.

posts.summary: Muratori posts a brief update explaining he couldn't write a full Witness Wednesday installment due to an unexpected time-critical Linux project. Instead, he shares a video demonstration of the Lister Panel UI element he had been detailing in previous posts. The original demo video couldn't be shared earlier due to game spoilers, so he recorded a new one in a spoiler-free area called CaseyTown. The post serves as a companion piece to his earlier technical write-ups about the Lister Panel code.
key_points: The Lister Panel is a UI element developed for The Witness that can display and navigate a large amount of game data
Spoiler concerns required recording a new demo video in a safe area rather than using the original team demo
The video is meant to contextualize the code walkthroughs from previous Witness Wednesday posts
money_quotes: The Lister Panel is capable of seeing a great many things.
This should help give you some perspective on what the code does when it's all put together.
posts.key_insight: Seeing a UI system in action provides essential context for understanding the code behind it.

posts.summary: Casey Muratori shares the complete ~2000 line source code for the Lister Panel from The Witness's level editor, inviting readers to examine it and submit questions for a Q&A session. He notes the code is a mixture of his and Jon Blow's coding conventions, adopted for maintainability. The code implements a sophisticated entity filtering, listing, and visualization system for the game's editor. Muratori emphasizes this is editor-only code where implementation cost is prioritized over robustness, since it won't ship to end users. The post serves as a rare window into production game development tooling on a major indie title.
key_points: The Lister Panel is a ~2000 line editor tool for The Witness that provides entity filtering, hierarchical listing, spatial queries, and debug visualization ('visors') for the level editor
Editor code deliberately prioritizes low implementation cost over robustness, since it's not user-facing — a pragmatic tradeoff that differs from shipping code standards
The code mixes two developers' conventions (Casey's and Jon Blow's) as a deliberate choice to ease maintenance for the project lead
The filtering system is extensible via a data-driven table of special filters with positive/negative/off tri-state modes, demonstrating practical game tool architecture
Debug visualization 'visors' provide spatial overlays (grass planting boundaries, light probe radii, entity positions) that are essential for content authoring in a 3D game
money_quotes: This is also editor-only code, which means it is not meant to be used in a released product, and thus the emphasis is entirely on keeping implementation costs low, and not on ancillary things like robustness that might be more of a priority in code meant for end users.
I adopted Jon's conventions in many places, just to make it easier for him to maintain.
Sometimes the answer will be, 'because I was in a hurry' :)
TODO (casey): WTF is this called toggle selection, when apparently it only clears the selection?
TODO (casey): WTF doesn't qsort pass a user parameter?
TODO (casey): Why can't I just _clear_ the hash table?
posts.key_insight: Editor and tooling code for games should prioritize low implementation cost and developer productivity over the robustness standards applied to shipping code, because the audience and failure consequences are fundamentally different.

posts.summary: Casey Muratori answers reader questions about the source code of The Witness editor's Lister Panel, which he previously shared publicly. He explains implementation details like row templates, comment conventions, serialization macros, and the immediate-mode GUI system. Along the way, he shares strong opinions on C++ templates/STL, object-oriented programming, code duplication versus abstraction, and his preference for permissive code design over restrictive practices. He also discusses his development environment, cross-platform work, and the historical reasons behind his distinctive coding style.
key_points: The STL and C++ templates represent one of the worst attempts at generic programming, causing ugly syntax, long compile times, hard-to-understand errors, and bloated symbol files
Object-oriented programming should basically never be used in C++, which lacks the features needed to make OOP beneficial — think in terms of algorithms first, not objects
He prefers permissive code design (macros over enums, minimal use of const/private) because the primary development cost lies in code interoperation, not debugging
Small code duplication is preferable to collapsed but harder-to-read logic — duplication becomes worth eliminating only when the shared code is intricate and likely to change
He uses differentiated comment keywords (NOTE, IMPORTANT, TODO, STUDY) to categorize comments by purpose rather than treating all comments the same
money_quotes: I think the STL is total poopsauce and I never use it in any of my projects either.
I have nothing positive to say about the STL and think it, combined with C++'s template specification, form one of the worst attempts at generic programming I've ever seen.
I usually prefer the option that supports the widest possible code use, whereas most C++ programmers seem to prefer the option that supports the narrowest.
I tend to find that the primary development cost in codebases to lie in working out the interoperation of large amounts of code, not in the debugging of that code.

I believe many modern programmers spend an inordinate amount of time using things like 'const' and 'private' to try to prevent possible misuses of a piece of code, all the while making the code less usable in a number of circumstances that would have worked just fine.
If you are programming in C++, you should basically never use object-oriented programming.
You should be thinking strictly in terms of the actual code and the algorithms that you are creating, and then you should build the backing data to fit those algorithms.
Sometimes I think they get together and have competitions to see who can make the shittier debugger, and QtCreator comes out on top, but its neck-and-neck right to the finish.
posts.key_insight: The primary cost in software development is working out code interoperation, not debugging, so programmers should favor permissive, flexible code over restrictive designs that save hypothetical debugging time at the expense of usability.

posts.summary: Muratori introduces a 2004 lecture on API design that he considers a landmark in establishing concrete, evaluable characteristics for reusable components. He recounts how the prevailing wisdom in game development held that only 'leaf technologies' (isolated, non-interdependent libraries) could succeed as middleware, and how Granny 3D's 1.x branch initially confirmed this belief. After a year of redesigning the API for the 2.x branch with Jeff Roberts, they cracked the problem of making deeply integrated systems reusable, shipping in over 2600 SKUs. The lecture codified five key characteristics of effective APIs, including the concept of 'integration discontinuity' as the primary pitfall to avoid.
key_points: API design is one of the most important and most neglected aspects of programming, with poor practices persisting regardless of technological advancement
The game industry believed only 'leaf technologies' (isolated, non-interdependent libraries) could succeed as reusable middleware, until Granny 2.x proved otherwise
The lecture introduced 'integration discontinuity' as a term for the primary pitfall APIs must avoid, and proposed concrete, observable characteristics for evaluating API quality
Granny 2.x's API design endured 12 years with minor modifications and shipped in 2600+ SKUs, demonstrating that deeply integrated systems can be made reusable with proper API design
Flow control emerged as an implicit but critical fifth characteristic of good API design, added on Chris Hecker's recommendation
money_quotes: To the best of my knowledge, it was the first (and last?) time anyone enumerated concrete, specific characteristics of APIs that could be directly observed and evaluated to determine whether an API would be effective in practice.
API design, for whatever reason, seems stuck in the past, regardless of how far the underlying technologies underneath advance.
The more thought and care that people put into an API's design up front, the more work is saved across the potentially hundreds of thousands of programmers who subsequently use it.

Character animation was definitely not a leaf technology, because it was a core element of how a game's rendering, physics, and gameplay code worked, so it always had to be tightly integrated with a number of interdependent systems.
Not wanting to admit defeat, Jeff Roberts and I spent over a year working out how we might design a new API for the 2.x branch that would finally crack the reusable component problem.
It's one of the only bodies of code I've ever written that I am truly proud to be associated with.
I've always been a little sad I never did a Granny 3.x, just to have one version that was designed from the very start with the complete understanding of what makes a great API.
posts.key_insight: Reusable APIs for deeply integrated systems can succeed where conventional wisdom says they cannot, but only if designed around concrete, evaluable characteristics that prevent integration discontinuities.

posts.summary: Muratori dissects the Event Tracing for Windows API as a case study in catastrophically bad API design. He first demonstrates what a clean API for the same task would look like — a few straightforward function calls to receive context switch events — then walks through the actual Microsoft implementation step by agonizing step. The real API requires massive grab-bag structures with undocumented magic numbers, manual memory arithmetic for string packing, elevated administrator privileges, and — most absurdly — a dedicated thread that blocks forever just to receive events via callback. The core argument is that writing usage code first would have prevented all of these design failures, since the underlying problem is trivially simple: copying structured data from kernel to user space.
key_points: Always write the usage code first when designing an API — this single practice reveals most design failures before they ship

The Event Tracing for Windows API turns what should be a glorified memcpy() into a gauntlet of memory arithmetic, string packing, magic numbers, redundant identifiers, elevated privileges, and a dedicated blocking thread
Microsoft's pattern of shared grab-bag parameter structures across function families destroys clarity about what goes into and comes out of each call
The API is internally inconsistent: StartTrace() and OpenTrace() handle errors, handles, and session names in completely different ways despite being part of the same system
Bad APIs impose compounding costs on hundreds of thousands of developers — API designers chronically underestimate this multiplier effect
money_quotes: I don't think people who make APIs really appreciate how important it is to get them right, and how much unnecessary work their mistakes can cause for hundreds, thousands, sometimes even millions of other programmers.

The API is simple because the problem it's solving is trivial. It's essentially the simplest API problem you can have in a system: how to move data from one place to another. It's a glorified memcpy().

But it is precisely the simplicity of the problem that allows the Event Tracing for Windows API to really shine. Even though all it has to do is move memory from one place to another, it manages to involve almost every kind of complexity you can see in an API.
Each time I figured out another step of the process I thought, 'Wait, seriously?' And each time Microsoft implicitly replied, 'Seriously.'
That's right — I am not exaggerating. If you simply want to receive a list of the context switches, even if only for your process, it must be running with full administrator privileges.
Here is the brown icing on the poopcake, that savory sauce that makes the Event Tracing for Windows API more grainy and aromatic than its contemporaries: ProcessTrace is defined to never return.
The only API I've ever used that requires both elevated privileges and a dedicated user thread just to copy a block of structures from the kernel to the user.

Without thinking about principles at all, or getting bogged down in details, just the simple exercise of writing down what the API should look like was all that was really necessary to see all the places the Microsoft version fell down.
posts.key_insight: Writing usage code first — imagining the ideal API from the caller's perspective before considering implementation — is the single most effective practice for preventing bad API design, because it immediately exposes unnecessary complexity.

posts.summary: Muratori identifies a fundamental design flaw in Windows OpenGL: the implicit coupling between OpenGL contexts and Windows device contexts, which makes multi-monitor and multi-window rendering unnecessarily painful. The original API design assumed single-thread, single-window usage, but modern applications need to target multiple windows across multiple monitors. The current workaround of calling wglMakeCurrent() repeatedly is both expensive and platform-dependent, breaking the otherwise cross-platform nature of OpenGL code. Muratori proposes a simple two-function extension (wglAcquireDCFramebuffer/wglReleaseDCFramebuffer) that leverages OpenGL's existing framebuffer API to make output targeting explicit rather than implicit. He also addresses the threading caveat by requiring fence synchronization when multiple contexts target the same framebuffer, keeping the driver simple.
key_points: OpenGL contexts on Windows implicitly bind to a single device context at creation time, a design decision that made sense for single-window apps but creates serious problems for multi-monitor rendering

wglMakeCurrent() is both expensive (drivers must do 'pattern discovery' to optimize flip-flopping) and platform-dependent, forcing otherwise cross-platform OpenGL code to thunk through the Windows platform layer

Multi-threaded rendering is further broken because two OpenGL contexts cannot legally target the same HDC simultaneously, requiring mutexes around wglMakeCurrent() even though the real serialization needed is only at GPU command retirement

The fix is just two new wgl functions that convert device contexts into framebuffer handles, reusing OpenGL's existing glBindFramebuffer mechanism that already solved the analogous problem for render-to-texture
Requiring explicit fence synchronization when multiple contexts share a framebuffer keeps drivers simple and gives applications control over command ordering
money_quotes: With the benefit of hindsight, I think it's fair to say that this implicit behavior was always a bad idea, it just never really caused that much of a problem because most early OpenGL usage involved a single thread targeting a single window on a single monitor.

wglMakeCurrent() is not assumed to be a lightweight operation on OpenGL drivers, and in general the OpenGL driver has no way of knowing a priori that all you're doing with the wglMakeCurrent() is flip-flopping between two consistently targeted windows.

If a driver wants to support this path efficiently, it introduces yet more 'pattern discovery' into the GPU driver as it tries to determine that the user is not actually targeting a fresh window requiring new setup.
All of these problems come from one root cause: the fact that OpenGL contexts implicitly target a single Windows device context. But there's no real reason that has to remain true.

The problem of how to tell the GL where output is supposed to go was already solved, and already works quite well. Thus all we really need to do is remove the implicit device context targeting of framebuffer handle 0 and replace it with explicit device context targeting with specific framebuffer handles.

Operations that target the window back buffer now must thunk to the platform layer and back in order to ensure that the magic 'target' of the operation has been set up before they can continue issuing what would otherwise have been platform-independent OpenGL code.
posts.key_insight: Implicit API couplings that seem harmless in simple use cases become architectural liabilities at scale, and the fix is often to make the implicit behavior explicit using mechanisms the API already provides.

posts.summary: Casey Muratori recounts the origin of Immediate-Mode Graphical User Interfaces (IMGUI), which he developed in 2002 while building a viewer utility for Granny 3D. He realized that retained-mode GUI design suffers from the same drawbacks as retained-mode graphics APIs, and found that an immediate-mode approach was simpler, more powerful, and handled 2D/3D interaction seamlessly. He coined the term 'Single-path Immediate Mode Graphical User Interface' and shared a rough public video in 2005. He notes the technique has since gained widespread adoption, even in web development, but expresses frustration that people argue about IMGUI without truly understanding it.
key_points: IMGUI was born from practical necessity: cross-platform GUI toolkits in 2002 were immature, forcing developers to roll their own solutions
The key insight was recognizing that retained-mode GUI code has the same drawbacks as retained-mode graphics APIs, and applying immediate-mode principles dramatically simplified UI programming
The technique has spread far beyond game development into web development and other disciplines
Muratori believes the simplicity of the IMGUI concept paradoxically causes people to think they understand it when they don't, leading to unproductive arguments
money_quotes: I began to realize that 'retained' GUI code design has all the same drawbacks that typically cause us to avoid using retained-mode APIs in graphics.

I experimented with having the GUI code operate in a more immediate fashion, like an immediate-mode graphics API, and found that everything was simpler, more powerful, and could span the gamut of both 2D and 3D interaction seamlessly.

I think this has something to do with how simple the IMGUI concept is, as it leads people to think they understand it, and then they proceed to get into heated arguments as if they actually know what they're talking about.
I rarely see this problem when I'm talking about anything even mildly complicated, like quaternions.
If you're arguing about it, you probably don't understand it very well.
There's really not a lot there to argue about.
posts.key_insight: Retained-mode GUI design suffers from the same fundamental problems as retained-mode graphics, and adopting an immediate-mode approach yields simpler, more powerful interfaces — a concept so simple that people routinely overcomplicate their understanding of it.

posts.summary: Muratori recounts his 2001 investigation into quaternion interpolation methods for the Granny 3D animation engine. Through building a visualization tool comparing linear, spherical linear, and exponential map interpolation, he discovered that linear and spherical interpolation of quaternions produce nearly identical paths, contrary to conventional wisdom. The half-angle construction of quaternions makes their linear interpolation speeds much closer to spherical interpolation than expected. This insight led him to treat quaternions as simple 4D vectors for polynomial combination, enabling order-independent animation compositing that was both faster and more flexible. He also discovered a technique exploiting quaternion double-cover (distinguishing 360 vs 720 degree rotations) to improve joint behavior during animation blending.
key_points: Linear and spherical interpolation of quaternions always trace the same path, and their speeds are far more similar than expected due to the half-angle construction
Quaternions can be treated as 4D vectors for polynomial combination, enabling order-independent animation compositing — contrary to what 'everyone knew' at the time
Exponential map interpolation diverges substantially from spherical interpolation at large rotations, while linear quaternion interpolation does not
Quaternion double-cover (distinguishing 360° from 720° rotations) can be exploited with preconditioning to produce more realistic joint behavior under animation compositing
Visualization-driven experimentation led to insights that the theoretical framing had obscured
money_quotes: As everyone 'knew' at the time, you could not treat them simply as 4D vectors that could be polynomially combined.
The more I played with it, the more I realized that I had been thinking about the problem all wrong.

Not only are linear and spherical interpolations of quaternions always exactly the same path, but because of the half-angle construction of quaternions, their speeds are much more similar than you would expect.
This visualization-driven realization informed the entire design of the 2.0 version Granny 3D animation engine.

Aided by a much better and more intuitive understanding of how quaternions really worked, I was able to develop a number of novel techniques that allowed the animation core to be much faster and more flexible.
Because quaternions differentiate between 360 and 720 degree rotations, with a little preconditioning you can make joints behave much more realistically.
posts.key_insight: Quaternion linear interpolation is far closer to spherical interpolation than conventionally believed, and this insight enables treating quaternions as simple 4D vectors for fast, order-independent animation compositing.

posts.summary: Muratori recounts how he came to implement an optimized version of the GJK intersection testing algorithm after a lunch conversation with Valve physicist Jay Stelly in 2004. Stelly explained that GJK papers were needlessly obtuse and that the algorithm was intuitive when understood geometrically rather than algebraically. Muratori implemented a solver based on this geometric insight and discovered that the required tests were much simpler than those in academic papers. He dubbed the optimized formulation 'GJKSM' and later presented it in video form and at the 2016 Papers We Love conference.
key_points: Academic GJK papers obfuscate a fundamentally simple algorithm by treating it purely algebraically rather than geometrically
A geometric understanding of GJK leads to significantly simpler and more optimal simplex tests than the standard algebraic formulation
The 2-simplex case can be reduced to zero checks, while the 4-simplex case remains an open optimization problem
Informal knowledge transfer between practitioners (a lunch conversation) produced better algorithmic insight than formal academic literature
money_quotes: He then proceeded to explain how both GJK and the expanding polytope algorithm for convex hulls were both very easy to understand when thought of geometrically.
Jay's explanation made perfect sense to me, and I found it intuitively obvious how to write a GJK solver from his explanation (unlike most GJK papers, which are violently obtuse).
I realized that the tests you needed to do for computing GJK, when considered geometrically, were much simpler than the ones advocated by the papers, which only considered the matter algebraically.

Although I generally don't like naming algorithms after their creators in lieu of a more properly descriptive name, if the original is already an absurd concatenation of names, why not continue the trend?
The 2-simplex case can be reduced to zero checks (like the 1-simplex case) because you already know the point is past the origin.
posts.key_insight: Thinking about algorithms geometrically rather than algebraically can reveal dramatically simpler implementations that academic papers miss by being trapped in their own formalism.

posts.summary: Muratori argues that all patents, but especially software patents, are fundamentally bad law with no empirical support for their existence. He explains that patents are not about preventing theft but about forcing people to pay for ideas even when independently conceived. Through a detailed email originally written to a Stanford Law student, he lays out six arguments against software patents: they're unusable in practice due to sheer volume, copyright already handles software licensing effectively, trivial concepts get patented while important ones don't, no one can define 'patent quality,' the pre-patent era was more innovative, and software patents raise First Amendment concerns. He concludes that after years of research and reading every Supreme Court case on the subject, he has found no evidence software patents are useful.
key_points: Patents are not about preventing theft — they force people to pay for ideas even when independently conceived, with triple damages only if the infringer knew about the patent
With over 40,000 software patents issued yearly, no programmer can know a priori whether they're infringing, making the system function purely as an uncertainty settlement market
Copyright already provides effective software licensing without patents — Muratori's own career earnings came from licensing unpatented software
Virtually everything we interact with on computers today was conceived before software patents existed in the pre-1990 era, suggesting patents haven't driven innovation
Software patents raise First Amendment issues because software is a medium of personal expression, making them analogous to patenting phrases in English
money_quotes: Patents are not about protecting intellectual property from theft — that is an almost negligible part of the law.
It's about forcing people to pay for the use of patented ideas even when they came up with the idea themselves.
