Almost the entire practical result from software patents is to create an uncertainty settlement market, whereby software developers pay patent holders after the fact to avoid entanglement in lawsuits.
It makes no sense to say that you need to 'fix the system' when none of the great stuff required it! Just get rid of the system, and you're done.
Whenever you sit down at a computer today, it is highly likely you will interact exclusively with things that are modified versions of something that existed in 1975.
Allowing patents on software is therefore much more similar to allowing patents on phrases or sentences in English, and raises all the same concerns.
I have found literally no evidence to ever suggest that software patents are useful, and plenty to show that they are actively harmful.
And I've yet to hear a coherent argument to the contrary that didn't fall apart immediately under a moment's scrutiny.
posts.key_insight: Software patents don't protect innovation — they create an uncertainty settlement market that taxes independent invention, while all the foundational software innovations were created without them.

posts.summary: Muratori questions why modern CPUs still use register-based instruction encodings when compilers and processors have both evolved past needing them literally. He argues that compilers naturally think in terms of memory operations and only deal with registers as an imposed constraint, while modern CPUs internally treat registers as abstract logical values anyway through out-of-order execution and register renaming. He proposes a stack-machine encoding where the top of the stack serves as a virtual register file, which could simplify context switching to saving just 3-4 values and remove artificial obfuscation between compiler and CPU. He acknowledges he may be missing something fundamental about hardware design and genuinely solicits answers from hardware engineers.
key_points: Compilers naturally reason about memory operations and only convert to register-based encodings because CPUs require it, adding unnecessary complexity to the compilation process

Modern CPUs already treat registers as abstract logical values through out-of-order execution and register renaming, so the register paradigm is a semantic fiction maintained at the instruction encoding level
A stack-relative addressing mode with compressed encodings could match register-based instruction sizes while eliminating the register abstraction entirely
Thread context switching would become trivially cheap — saving 3-4 values instead of the entire register file — if registers were just memory locations at the top of the stack
The real constraint may be that allowing registers to be addressable by pointers would create pipeline hazards, but this could be handled similarly to branch mispredictions
money_quotes: Compilers only care about registers because they have to.

By adding the constraint that most operations have to go through registers to happen, the current paradigm effectively adds a bunch of obfuscation from the compiler to the CPU, making it harder for the CPU to tell what is actually necessary.
When you talk about a 'register' in a modern CPU, you're really talking about a 'unique transient value' anyway!
A 'register' is now a semantic idea first, and an actual physical thing second.
The compiler knows what all the unique values are, and where they are in memory. The CPU needs to know that, too, and just wants to do the right series of operations.

If all register-esque operations were instead encoded as memory operations at the top of the stack, swapping threads would always be a matter of saving and restoring just 3 64-bit values, no matter how complex the processor's logic.
I asked some engineers at Intel the innocent question, 'Why is memory latency so high?' The answer was simply that the memory was literally far away from the CPU.
posts.key_insight: The register abstraction in modern CPUs may be an anachronistic encoding convention that adds complexity for both compilers and processors, since both sides already reason about values abstractly rather than in terms of physical register locations.

posts.summary: Muratori describes a 2007 lecture he gave at MIT's GAMBIT game lab about the importance of understanding programming and math at a deep level rather than treating tools as black boxes. Even before starting Handmade Hero, he was concerned about newer programmers lacking core low-level skills. He re-recorded the lecture in 2018 for his Handmade Hero stream, and notes that a discussion of normal map generation complexities would further illustrate why deep understanding matters.
key_points: Newer generations of programmers increasingly lack core, low-level programming skills
Understanding programming and math at a deep level is critical rather than just learning to use premade tools as abstract black boxes
The complexities behind normal map generation serve as another example of why deep technical understanding matters
Muratori's concerns about abstraction-dependent programming predated Handmade Hero by several years
money_quotes: I was already feeling troubled by the lack of core, low-level programming skills I was seeing in most newer generations of programmers.

I wanted to try giving a lecture that explained in more detail why it was so important for people to understand programming (and math) at a deep level, and not just learn to use premade things as abstract tools.
posts.key_insight: Programmers who treat tools as opaque black boxes without understanding the underlying programming and math fundamentals are fundamentally limited in their ability to solve real problems.

posts.summary: Muratori describes his 2016 talk at the inaugural Papers We Love conference, where he was invited as the sole game industry representative. He discussed how academic research papers influenced his game technology work, selecting Marching Cubes, Quaternions, and the Gilbert-Johnson-Keerthi distance algorithm as his focal papers. He reveals that Marching Cubes played an integral role in the robust player movement code he developed for The Witness, though he hasn't yet given a detailed talk explaining how.
key_points: Muratori was the sole game industry representative at the inaugural Papers We Love conference, highlighting the gap between academic CS and game development communities
He selected three foundational papers — Marching Cubes, Quaternions, and GJK distance computation — as examples of research that directly inspired game technology work
Marching Cubes, typically associated with volumetric rendering, was repurposed for robust player movement code in The Witness, suggesting creative cross-domain application of research
The talk examined how academic research did or did not play a significant role in practical game development
money_quotes: As the sole representative of the game industry at the conference, for the lecture topic they requested that I look back at my programming career and discuss how research either did or did not play a significant role in inspiring interesting game technology work.
Marching Cubes played an integral role in the solution to the robust player movement code I developed for The Witness.
posts.key_insight: Academic research papers, even from seemingly unrelated domains like volumetric rendering, can directly inspire creative solutions to practical game development problems like player movement.

posts.summary: Muratori argues that while computing hardware has improved dramatically since 1990, software reliability has actually deteriorated. He identifies the lack of platform competition—duopolies on both desktop and phone—as a key driver, noting that OS vendors no longer treat operating systems as products but as vehicles for other business goals. The complexity of interfacing with consumer hardware creates an insurmountable barrier to new OS entrants, which also explains Linux's struggles on the desktop. He proposes that standardizing on stable instruction set architectures (ISAs) could be a first step toward fixing what he calls 'The Thirty Million Line Problem'—the overwhelming complexity that undermines software quality.
key_points: Software reliability has gotten worse since 1990 despite massive hardware improvements—mandatory updates, malware, ads, and instability are now the norm
Desktop and phone markets are each controlled by duopolies whose revenue doesn't depend on OS quality, so they optimize for other business goals instead of reliability
The complexity of hardware interfacing creates an insurmountable barrier to new operating systems, locking in the duopoly and explaining Linux's desktop struggles
Stable, simple ISAs could reduce the thirty-million-line driver/OS complexity and allow reliability-focused developers to build better software stacks
money_quotes: While features and variety have improved, trust and reliability has, if anything, hit an all-time low.
Unlike that of yesteryear, software working in the morning today may well suddenly stop working in the afternoon, even if the user has done absolutely nothing in the interim.

The operating system is never a product anymore — it is merely something users are forced to use based on the hardware they have chosen, and it is increasingly treated solely as a vehicle for pursuing the platform holders' other business goals.

The unchallenged duopoly on both major computing form factors is likely due to the fact that interfacing with consumer hardware is now so difficult that it would be impossible for any new operating system to enter the market.
While shipping a stable ISA is more expensive that shipping continually updated buggy drivers, the rewards both to hardware companies and to users could be significant.
posts.key_insight: The complexity of modern hardware interfaces has created an unbreakable OS duopoly that prioritizes business goals over reliability, and only simplifying hardware abstraction through stable ISAs can break the cycle.

posts.summary: Muratori describes a lecture he gave at Busan Indie Connect 2018 about the player movement system he developed for The Witness. The lecture details novel algorithms that provide real-time, precomputation-free, provably robust player movement. It follows up on his earlier 2012 'Walk Monster' article about making player movement more robust. The post is primarily a reference page for the lecture video, noting that the original recording had severe audio issues but an improved version using simultaneous translation recordings was later created.
key_points: The Witness's final movement system used novel algorithms for real-time, precomputation-free, provably robust player movement
The lecture covers motivation, implementation overview, and practical examples from both the game and editor
This work evolved over at least six years from the initial 2012 Walk Monster article to the 2018 lecture
The original recording had severe audio problems due to the venue's inexperienced A/V setup
money_quotes: The final movement system shipped in The Witness used several novel algorithms to provide real-time, precomputation-free, provably robust player movement.
This lecture gives the motivation behind the system, an overview of its implementation, and examples of how it is used in both the game and the editor.
posts.key_insight: The Witness's player movement system was solved through novel algorithms that achieved provably robust, real-time movement without precomputation, representing years of iterative development from initial problem identification to final shipped solution.

posts.summary: Muratori evaluates whether WinDBG Preview has improved enough to replace Visual Studio as his everyday debugger. He documents Visual Studio's steady decline since version 6, noting its painfully slow watch window, inability to show out-of-scope variables, and bloated installation process. After testing WinDBG Preview, he concludes it is now viable as a daily debugger, which he considers great news for developers suffering under Visual Studio's deterioration. He provides a detailed list of remaining usability issues — keyboard shortcuts, breakpoint management, register views, and configuration handling — as constructive feedback to Microsoft. The post serves as both a practical review and a plea for Microsoft to invest in polishing WinDBG's usability to make it a definitive Visual Studio replacement.
key_points: Visual Studio's debugger has deteriorated significantly since version 6, with slow watch windows, multi-second project switching, and inability to inspect out-of-scope variables
WinDBG is actually more powerful than Visual Studio's debugger, is much smaller, and can be run by simply copying a directory — no bloated installer required
WinDBG Preview has crossed the threshold into daily usability, but still needs keyboard shortcut improvements, better breakpoint naming, and decoupled UI/target configuration
The gap between WinDBG and everyday use is primarily UX friction — too many manual steps for common operations like re-running executables and setting breakpoints by name
Microsoft distributing WinDBG only through the Windows Store is a missed opportunity; a ZIP-based distribution would prevent the installer bloat that plagued Visual Studio
money_quotes: The debugger in Visual Studio 2017 is a mess.

The watch window takes seconds to update even for simple displays, and it doesn't even show you that it's busy refreshing, so if the values don't change, you don't even know if it's done updating or not!
In many ways, WinDBG is actually the better debugger, even for non-kernel debugging.
Unlike Visual Studio, it doesn't require a huge several-minute bloated installation process. You can just copy the WinDBG directory to a machine, and it will run.
I'm delighted to report that, although it didn't seem like it at first, once I dug in, WinDBG Preview really does appear to be usable as an everyday debugger!
This is great news for everyone who's been chafing under Visual Studio's deteriorating debugger for the past fifteen years.
Committing to such a deployment would ensure that bloated installer creep couldn't happen (like it has for Visual Studio).
I'm already thinking about ditching Visual Studio for good and using WinDBG exclusively.
posts.key_insight: WinDBG Preview has finally crossed the usability threshold for daily debugging, and with modest UX polish it could fully replace Visual Studio's increasingly degraded debugger.

posts.summary: Muratori documents the absurd workaround required to make clang++ on Windows find Microsoft's link.exe linker. Rather than providing a simple command-line switch to specify the linker path, clang has ~1500 lines of code dedicated to 'finding' the linker by detecting Visual Studio installations. The only reliable solution is to reverse-engineer that detection code and construct a fake Visual Studio install directory structure. Muratori provides the minimal steps: create a fake bin/HostX64/x64 directory, copy link.exe into it, set VCToolsInstallDir, and add the real linker path to PATH for DLL resolution. He wraps everything in a batch file with setlocal to avoid environment contamination.
key_points: Clang on Windows has no command-line switch to specify the linker path directly, despite this being the obvious solution
Instead, clang has ~1500 lines of code that tries to auto-detect Visual Studio installations by matching specific directory structures
The workaround requires constructing a fake Visual Studio directory tree and setting VCToolsInstallDir to point clang at it
Muratori wraps the solution in a batch file using setlocal to keep environment variables isolated, allowing cl and clang++ to coexist without vcvarsall.bat
money_quotes: In a sane world, clang++ would have a command line switch that worked like this: clang++ --linkexe="w:/apps/msvc/x64/link.exe" test.cpp
That is not the world we live in, and so instead there is no such switch, and clang has around 1500 lines of source code dedicated to 'finding' where the linker is.
The only way to point clang++ at a specific link.exe and guarantee that it will work is to reverse engineer the 1500 lines of code and then construct a fake Visual Studio install for it to 'find'.
The clang code for finding the linker is extremely janky.
I would not recommend allowing either of those paths to have spaces, or otherwise require quoting, because they may very well not work.
Similarly, I have a cl.bat which does exactly the same thing, it's just much simpler because it doesn't need the handholding to find link.exe.
posts.key_insight: Clang's auto-detection of Visual Studio installations replaces a trivial command-line flag with 1500 lines of brittle heuristics, forcing users to reverse-engineer the detection logic and build fake directory structures just to specify a linker path.

posts.summary: Muratori describes designing a 'stable' half-pixel filter for video compression that avoids the feedback artifacts produced by standard filters like those in h.264 and HEVC. Standard half-pel filters used in video codecs either blur the image (bilinear) or introduce sharpening artifacts that compound destructively when applied repeatedly across frames. He discovered that existing filters in major codecs create feedback loops where sharpening artifacts magnify with each application, eventually destroying the image. With no prior filter design experience, he found coefficients that sit on the boundary between sharpening and blurring, converging to a stable image that doesn't degrade further even after a million applications. He notes this 'stable filtering' concept appears absent from video compression literature despite seeming obviously important.
key_points: Standard half-pel filters in h.264 and HEVC use negative weights for sharpening, which creates destructive feedback loops when applied repeatedly across video frames
Bilinear filtering avoids sharpening artifacts but introduces cumulative blur, while codec filters produce increasingly severe ringing artifacts with each application
Muratori's filter kernel sits at the boundary between sharpening and blurring, converging to a stable representation that stops degrading after roughly 100 applications
The practical motivation is reducing memory requirements in video codecs — stable filters avoid needing to store multiple reference frames to work around half-pel degradation
Despite the problem's practical importance, Muratori found no discussion of filter stability under repeated application in mainstream video compression literature
money_quotes: Where bilinear filtering reduces the sharpness in an image, these common filters enhance it. They are actually sharpening the image, just like the "sharpen" operation in your favorite photo editing package might do.

Because this sharpening is procedural and based on the differences between pixels, it creates a feedback loop that will continue to sharpen the same edge over and over and over until it destroys the image.

I had never looked at filters before at all — literally. I had no idea how people typically designed them. Strangely enough, this turned out to be a good thing, because it meant I looked at the problem without a lot of preconceived notions.

A filter is "stable" if it does not feed back on itself, so it can be applied over and over and not produce artifacts. A filter is "unstable" if it produces artifacts that magnify with successive applications, eventually destroying the image.
The concept of "feedback" is well established in audio, but it just doesn't seem to be a central concern in image processing, perhaps because filters are normally only meant to be applied once?

This kernel is on the edge between sharpening and blurring. Because sharpening will always feed back to bright, obvious artifacts, this filter kernel prefers slight blurring so that the image will just appear "duller".
After 300 frames, every filter other than mine is a joke.
Although some blurring is introduced in the first hundred or so applications, it appears to converge to a stable image representation that never degrades thereafter.
posts.key_insight: Standard video codec half-pel filters contain hidden sharpening that creates destructive feedback loops under repeated application, but a filter tuned to the boundary between sharpening and blurring can converge to a stable image that never degrades further.

posts.summary: In this second part of a two-part series, Muratori refines his approach to finding 'infinitely stable' half-pixel filters — filters that can be applied repeatedly without degrading the image. He builds a proper test harness that corrects for pixel shift, enabling automated convergence testing, and confirms that standard codec filters (h.264, HEVC, Lanczos) all 'explode' while his original integer filter remains stable. He then relaxes the original constraint of small-integer-only coefficients and discovers superior floating-point filters — first a 6-tap version that's nearly indistinguishable from the original image, then a remarkable 8-tap version that converges faster and preserves even more detail. He closes by noting that eigenvalue analysis can explain why these filters work, pointing readers to Fabian Giesen's detailed mathematical treatment.
key_points: A corrected test harness that shifts the image back after each pair of filter applications enables automated convergence testing and precise comparison against the original image.
All standard half-pel filters (bilinear, h.264, HEVC, Lanczos) eventually 'explode' under repeated application, while Muratori's integer filter converges to a stable image after 208 iterations.

The original integer filter was constrained to small integers over power-of-two divisors for hardware efficiency — relaxing this to arbitrary floating-point coefficients yields dramatically sharper stable filters.

An 8-tap floating-point filter found by searching between the stable integer filter and unstable Lanczos produces results nearly indistinguishable from the original image while remaining infinitely stable.
The stability properties can be analyzed through eigenvalue/eigenvector analysis of the filter, though the rounding to finite bit depth complicates the pure algebraic picture.
money_quotes: Spoiler alert: the answer is "probably not" and "definitely, here it is".
We now know, at least for a wide range of relevant test images, that this filter is actually infinitely stable, meaning that it will never produce an artifact no matter how many times you apply it.

Because I was targeting simple 16-bit integer operations only, I looked exclusively at kernels that could be expressed with small integers over power-of-two divisors up to 64, and no more. This is a much more constrained set of filters than if I had been looking at any set of 6 floating point coefficients.

You can fit two 16-bit integers into the space of one 32-bit float, effectively doubling your operational throughput, so depending on the exact cycle counts of the operations you need in floating point vs. integer, floating point isn't necessarily the fastest option.
Intuitively we should be able to find the place in-between the two sets of coefficients where degradation starts.
As an infinitely-stable filter, it performs shockingly well. It converges after 202 iterations, and looks so much like the original that it can be hard to guess which one is which.
It turns out there is literally eigenvector and eigenvalue analysis for filters, it's just not quite done the same way.
posts.key_insight: By searching the space between a known-stable integer filter and sharper but unstable Lanczos filters, you can find floating-point filter coefficients that are both infinitely stable under repeated application and nearly indistinguishable from the original image.

posts.summary: Muratori introduces Prime Pussify, a Photoshop script he created for the comic Meow the Infinite that automatically jitters font attributes on a per-character basis. The script solves the problem that Photoshop's built-in text tools are too limited to easily randomize character properties like baseline, size, and spacing. He argues that adding jitter programmatically is superior to using fonts with built-in jitter, since those tend to look repetitive with identical letter sequences. The post serves as a practical tool announcement with usage instructions and a note about Photoshop's scripting limitations.
key_points: Photoshop's text effect tools are too limited to efficiently jitter individual character attributes, requiring manual per-character adjustments
Fonts with built-in jitter look repetitive because identical letter sequences produce identical appearances, making programmatic randomization superior
The script randomizes baseline, size, and spacing per character, and can be re-run repeatedly to find a pleasing randomization
Photoshop's scripting API is severely limited — scripts cannot create real UI elements, only basic text input dialogs
money_quotes: Fonts that are jittery by design often look too repetitive, since the same letter sequences will tend to look the same, so it's usually better to add jitter than to build it into the font anyway.
But Photoshop's text tools are extremely limited, and don't allow you to quickly accomplish an effect like this.
To really jitter a line of text, you'd have to manually select each letter and change the font properties one by one.
Unfortunately, Photoshop is very janky.
As far as I can tell, Photoshop does not allow scripts to pop up any actual UI.
posts.key_insight: When creative tools lack the automation you need, writing a simple script to randomize per-character attributes produces better results than relying on pre-built jittery fonts or tedious manual adjustments.

posts.summary: Muratori draws an extended analogy between modern printing presses and GPUs, inspired by his prepress work on the book Meow the Infinite. He maps GPU concepts like frame rates, multi-channel rendering, texture caches, pipeline stalls, and debug instrumentation onto their printing press equivalents. The post is deliberately impractical — a self-aware Buzzfeed-style listicle meant purely to convey enthusiasm for how impressive industrial printing technology is. He notes that web presses have fixed 'frame rates' measured in feet per second, use plate-based 'texture caches' that cause pipeline stalls on swap, and employ high-speed cameras as debug instrumentation.
key_points: Web presses operate on continuous paper feeds at fixed rates, analogous to fixed frame rate rendering, while sheet-fed presses allow variable processing per sheet
Printing presses use multi-channel rendering with four or more inking assemblies in series, and sheet-fed presses can even do multi-pass rendering by re-feeding sheets
Physical plates act as a texture cache — swapping them causes a full pipeline stall, and no one appears to have built hot-swappable plate systems for web presses
Heat-set inks require paper to pass through long ovens at precise speeds, directly analogous to pipeline latency and inserted delays in GPU processing
High-speed cameras on press lines serve the same diagnostic role as GPU debug capture tools
money_quotes: If the only tool you have is an analogy to a hammer, every problem looks like a story about nails.
If Buzzfeed can make lists of things that nobody cares about, so can I.
The more you learn about these things, the more you realize how awesome they are, and you want to keep going.
It is literally just a list of ways that printing presses are like GPUs. There's no point to it whatsoever, except to try to impress upon the uninitiated just how cool modern printing presses are.
Remember back when texture changes were really expensive, and the GPU could only read a few textures at once? Well, modern printing presses are exactly like that.
If, by the time you reach the end of this list, you are motivated to watch a YouTube video on industrial web offset printing, my work here is done.
It's kind of ridiculous how accurate offset presses can be, especially when you consider that web presses are operating on paper at speeds of several feet per second.
posts.key_insight: Modern industrial printing presses are remarkably analogous to GPUs in their architecture — from fixed-rate pipelines and multi-channel rendering to texture caches, pipeline stalls, and debug instrumentation — revealing that the fundamental engineering challenges of high-throughput image production transcend the digital/physical divide.
